methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
ACSL, a Tokyo-listed public company in the Drones & Autonomous sector founded by Kenzo Nonami, presents a limited peer universe for valuation triangulation. The addressable comp set consists of six private firms in the sector—including Zipline, Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, and Elroy Air—with zero public trading comparables available for direct benchmarking.

The Peer-Median method is not applicable due to insufficient narrow peer clustering. While five same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, filtering by stage, proximity, and valuation tier yields fewer than three datapoints needed for reliable median analysis. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material methodological distortion given ACSL's specific positioning within autonomous systems.

This analysis underscores a fundamental constraint: ACSL's post-IPO, Japan-domiciled status places it in a sparse intersection of stage and geography, limiting direct comparable company triangulation. Valuation support would require either (1) alternative methods such as DCF or precedent transaction analysis, or (2) expansion to adjacent sectors with sufficient public and private depth. The absence of quantitative triangulation results means a valuation range cannot be derived from peer data alone. Key uncertainties include ACSL's revenue trajectory, margin profile relative to private peers, and market-specific discount factors for Tokyo listing versus USD-denominated comparables.
